herbs.effect: Both lower blood sugar — combined use may cause hypoglycemia
herbs.recommendation: Monitor blood glucose closely. Reduce fenugreek dose or use only culinary amounts. Inform doctor.
herbs.alternative: Karela (bitter gourd) in smaller amounts is slightly safer

herbs.effect: Additive blood glucose lowering — risk of hypoglycemia, especially if Metformin dose is high
herbs.recommendation: Monitor blood sugar twice daily. May need Metformin dose reduction by prescriber.

herbs.effect: Gymnema directly stimulates insulin secretion AND blocks sugar absorption — additive hypoglycemia risk is significant
herbs.recommendation: AVOID combining without physician supervision. If used, halve Gymnema dose and monitor glucose 2 hrs post-meal.
herbs.alternative: Berberine (not classical Ayurveda but safer alternative) — inform doctor

herbs.effect: Neem has demonstrated hypoglycemic activity — additive effect with Metformin
herbs.recommendation: Use only culinary amounts. Avoid Neem capsule/extract supplements on Metformin.

herbs.effect: Amla's high Vitamin C content may affect glucose metabolism and Metformin absorption timing
herbs.recommendation: Safe in culinary and normal Rasayana doses. Separate high-dose Amla from Metformin by 2 hours.

herbs.effect: High-dose cinnamon supplements enhance insulin sensitivity — additive effect
herbs.recommendation: Culinary amounts (1/4-1/2 tsp daily) are safe. Avoid cinnamon capsules >1g/day on Metformin.

herbs.effect: No significant glucose interaction at standard doses
herbs.recommendation: Safe to use at 300-600mg. Monitor if unusually high doses used.

herbs.effect: Ashwagandha may lower blood glucose via cortisol reduction — additive with insulin, risk of hypoglycemia
herbs.recommendation: Monitor blood glucose more frequently. Inform endocrinologist. May need insulin dose reduction.

herbs.effect: Shilajit enhances insulin sensitivity and glucose uptake — additive hypoglycemic risk
herbs.recommendation: Monitor blood sugar closely. Avoid shilajit without informing diabetologist.

herbs.effect: Gymnema stimulates endogenous insulin secretion — combined with exogenous insulin poses serious hypoglycemia risk
herbs.recommendation: AVOID. Gymnema + insulin combination requires intensive medical supervision.

herbs.effect: Additive blood glucose lowering — monitor carefully
herbs.recommendation: Use only culinary amounts while on insulin. Avoid concentrated juices or supplements.

herbs.effect: Galactomannan fiber slows glucose absorption — changes insulin timing requirements
herbs.recommendation: If using fenugreek seeds with meals, test blood sugar timing carefully. May need pre-meal insulin timing adjustment.

herbs.effect: Tulsi has demonstrated hypoglycemic properties — additive with sulfonylureas, hypoglycemia risk
herbs.recommendation: Use only culinary amounts of fresh Tulsi leaves. Avoid Tulsi extract supplements on Glipizide.

herbs.effect: Additive glucose lowering
herbs.recommendation: Avoid Neem supplements. Culinary use only.

herbs.effect: Additive hypoglycemic effect
herbs.recommendation: Monitor blood glucose. Inform prescriber.

herbs.effect: Garlic inhibits platelet aggregation and has anticoagulant properties — significantly increases bleeding risk and raises INR
herbs.recommendation: AVOID garlic supplements or high-dose garlic while on Warfarin. Culinary amounts (1-2 cloves in cooking) generally safe — monitor INR monthly.
herbs.alternative: Arjuna for cardiovascular support is safer than garlic on Warfarin

herbs.effect: Ginger inhibits thromboxane synthesis and platelet aggregation — increases anticoagulation and bleeding risk
herbs.recommendation: AVOID ginger supplements or ginger tea exceeding 1g/day on Warfarin. Culinary ginger in cooking is acceptable.

herbs.effect: Curcumin inhibits platelet aggregation and potentiates Warfarin — significant INR elevation documented
herbs.recommendation: AVOID turmeric/curcumin supplements on Warfarin. Culinary turmeric (1/4 tsp in cooking) is generally safe. Monitor INR.
herbs.alternative: Continue culinary turmeric only — no supplements

herbs.effect: Guggulsterones affect CYP2C9 enzyme that metabolises Warfarin — unpredictable INR changes
herbs.recommendation: AVOID Guggulu preparations while on Warfarin without weekly INR monitoring.

herbs.effect: Haritaki component has antiplatelet activity; tannins may affect Warfarin absorption
herbs.recommendation: Avoid Triphala while on Warfarin. Use psyllium husk for constipation instead.
herbs.alternative: Psyllium husk (Isabgol) is safe on Warfarin

herbs.effect: Amla has antiplatelet activity and high Vitamin C may affect Warfarin metabolism
herbs.recommendation: Avoid high-dose Amla supplements. Dietary Amla (1 fruit daily, Chyawanprash) is generally acceptable.

herbs.effect: No significant Warfarin interaction documented
herbs.recommendation: Safe to use at standard doses. Monitor INR if starting any new herb.

herbs.effect: Additive antiplatelet effect — combined use increases bleeding risk
herbs.recommendation: Culinary amounts generally safe. Avoid high-dose garlic supplements on Aspirin.

herbs.effect: Additive antiplatelet effect
herbs.recommendation: Culinary ginger acceptable. Avoid ginger supplements > 500mg/day on Aspirin.

herbs.effect: Additive antiplatelet activity
herbs.recommendation: Culinary use safe. Avoid curcumin supplements > 1g/day on low-dose Aspirin therapy.

herbs.effect: Additive antiplatelet effects — major bleeding risk
herbs.recommendation: AVOID garlic supplements on Clopidogrel. Inform cardiologist.

herbs.effect: Additive antiplatelet effects
herbs.recommendation: AVOID ginger supplements. Culinary cooking amounts only.

herbs.effect: Additive antiplatelet, may affect CYP2C19 activation of Clopidogrel
herbs.recommendation: Avoid turmeric supplements. Culinary use acceptable.

herbs.effect: May enhance blood pressure lowering — additive hypotensive effect, especially in high doses
herbs.recommendation: Use lower Ashwagandha dose (300mg). Monitor BP regularly. Generally safe.

herbs.effect: Garlic has antihypertensive properties — additive effect possible
herbs.recommendation: Culinary amounts fine. Avoid high-dose garlic supplements.

herbs.effect: Licorice contains glycyrrhizin which causes sodium retention and raises blood pressure — directly counteracts ACE inhibitor and worsens hypertension
herbs.recommendation: STRICTLY AVOID licorice root (Yashtimadhu) while on Lisinopril or any antihypertensive. DGL (deglycyrrhizinated licorice) removes glycyrrhizin and is safer.
herbs.alternative: DGL (deglycyrrhizinated licorice) removes the BP-raising component

herbs.effect: Punarnava has diuretic properties — combined with ACE inhibitor may cause additive BP lowering and electrolyte imbalance
herbs.recommendation: Monitor BP and potassium levels if combining. Reduce Punarnava dose.

herbs.effect: May interfere with drug absorption via CYP interaction
herbs.recommendation: Take Guggulu 2+ hours away from Lisinopril dose.

herbs.effect: Arjuna has calcium channel blocking properties and cardiac glycoside-like activity — additive hypotensive and cardiac effects. Narrow therapeutic overlap.
herbs.recommendation: AVOID Arjuna while on Amlodipine without cardiologist oversight. Monitor BP and heart rate closely if used.

herbs.effect: Glycyrrhizin raises BP and counteracts antihypertensive therapy
herbs.recommendation: STRICTLY AVOID. Even herbal teas containing licorice.

herbs.effect: May enhance BP lowering — monitor
herbs.recommendation: Generally safe. Monitor BP when starting.

herbs.effect: Arjuna has beta-blocker-like effects on cardiac conduction — additive bradycardia and hypotension risk
herbs.recommendation: Avoid combining without cardiologist supervision. Monitor resting heart rate (keep > 55 bpm).

herbs.effect: Mild additive BP lowering
herbs.recommendation: Monitor BP. Generally safe at 300mg.

herbs.effect: Punarnava is a potent Ayurvedic diuretic (Mutrala) — additive diuresis with Furosemide may cause excessive fluid loss and electrolyte imbalance (low potassium, sodium)
herbs.recommendation: AVOID combining. If used, monitor serum electrolytes weekly and stay well-hydrated.
herbs.alternative: Reduce Punarnava to very low dose and monitor closely

herbs.effect: Gokshura has diuretic properties — additive effect with loop diuretic
herbs.recommendation: Monitor electrolytes. Avoid concentrated Gokshura extracts.

herbs.effect: Licorice causes potassium loss; Furosemide also causes potassium loss — risk of severe hypokalemia (dangerous arrhythmia)
herbs.recommendation: STRICTLY AVOID this combination. Risk of life-threatening hypokalemia.

herbs.effect: Ashwagandha may increase T3 and T4 levels — risk of over-medication symptoms (palpitations, sweating, insomnia) in hypothyroid patients on Levothyroxine
herbs.recommendation: Consult endocrinologist before adding Ashwagandha. Check thyroid function after 6 weeks. May need Levothyroxine dose reduction.

herbs.effect: Kanchanar Guggulu is the primary Ayurvedic thyroid herb — may alter thyroid function and affect Levothyroxine requirements
herbs.recommendation: Separate Kanchanar Guggulu from Levothyroxine by at least 4 hours (thyroid meds must be taken on empty stomach). Monitor thyroid levels every 3 months.

herbs.effect: Guggulsterone directly affects thyroid function — may increase or decrease T3/T4
herbs.recommendation: AVOID Guggulu while on thyroid medication without endocrinologist supervision.

herbs.effect: Tannins in Triphala may reduce Levothyroxine absorption
herbs.recommendation: Separate by at least 4 hours from morning Levothyroxine dose. Take Triphala at night.

herbs.effect: No significant thyroid interaction documented
herbs.recommendation: Safe to use.

herbs.effect: Both affect GABAergic and serotonin pathways — potential additive CNS depression and sedation
herbs.recommendation: Use with caution. Start at low dose (150mg). Monitor for excessive drowsiness, sedation, or mood changes.

herbs.effect: Brahmi affects serotonergic neurotransmission — may potentiate SSRI effects or cause additive side effects
herbs.recommendation: Consult psychiatrist before combining. Monitor for serotonin syndrome symptoms (restlessness, rapid heart rate, tremor).

herbs.effect: Jatamansi has serotonin-modulating properties — additive CNS and serotonergic effects possible
herbs.recommendation: Avoid combining without psychiatric supervision. Jatamansi + SSRI risks serotonin syndrome in susceptible individuals.

herbs.effect: No significant SSRI interaction
herbs.recommendation: Safe to use.

herbs.effect: Mild CNS activity — monitor for additive sedation
herbs.recommendation: Generally safe at normal doses. Avoid high doses.

herbs.effect: Additive CNS depression — see fluoxetine entry
herbs.recommendation: Start low, monitor closely. Inform psychiatrist.

herbs.effect: Additive serotonergic effects
herbs.recommendation: Consult psychiatrist. Monitor for serotonin syndrome symptoms.

herbs.effect: MAO-inhibitory properties of Tulsi — mild interaction with SSRIs possible at very high doses
herbs.recommendation: Culinary and normal tea amounts are safe. Avoid concentrated Tulsi extract supplements.

herbs.effect: Pippali contains piperine which inhibits MAO — additive MAO inhibition with MAOI drugs risks hypertensive crisis, especially if tyramine-containing foods also consumed
herbs.recommendation: STRICTLY AVOID piperine-containing herbs (Pippali, Maricha, Trikatu) on MAOI antidepressants.

herbs.effect: Stimulant-like effects of Ashwagandha may interact with MAOIs — risk of hypertensive crisis or serotonin syndrome
herbs.recommendation: AVOID Ashwagandha on MAOI drugs.

herbs.effect: Serotonergic and dopaminergic effects of Brahmi may cause serotonin syndrome with MAOIs
herbs.recommendation: STRICTLY AVOID Brahmi on MAOI antidepressants.

herbs.effect: Stimulant herbs can precipitate hypertensive crisis on MAOIs
herbs.recommendation: AVOID all stimulant Rasayana herbs (Shilajit, Ashwagandha, Kapikachhu) on MAOIs.

herbs.effect: CRITICAL — Documented reduction in Phenobarbital serum levels by Shankhapushpi preparations (case reports). Shankhapushpi induces CYP enzymes, increasing Phenobarbital clearance — risk of seizure breakthrough.
herbs.recommendation: STRICTLY CONTRAINDICATED. Never use Shankhapushpi (or preparations containing it like Saraswatarishta) while on Phenobarbital. This is the most clinically documented herb-drug interaction in Ayurveda.
herbs.alternative: Brahmi alone (without Shankhapushpi) has less interaction; use Jatamansi for anxiety

herbs.effect: Additive CNS depression — combined sedation may be excessive
herbs.recommendation: Avoid or use at very low doses under neurologist guidance.

herbs.effect: Additive CNS depression
herbs.recommendation: Use with caution under supervision.

herbs.effect: Both potentiate GABA — combined use causes excessive CNS depression, respiratory depression risk at high doses
herbs.recommendation: AVOID combining without psychiatric supervision. If used, Ashwagandha should be very low dose (150mg) and benzodiazepine dose may need reduction.

herbs.effect: Additive sedation and cognitive slowing — may impair driving, concentration
herbs.recommendation: Avoid combination. If essential, use only at night and do not drive.

herbs.effect: Additive CNS depression
herbs.recommendation: Avoid combining. Monitor for excessive sedation.

herbs.effect: Tagara (Valeriana wallichii) is the Ayurvedic equivalent of Valerian — additive benzodiazepine-like CNS depression
herbs.recommendation: AVOID Tagara/Valerian with benzodiazepines — risk of respiratory depression at high doses.

herbs.effect: Additive CNS depression and sedation
herbs.recommendation: Avoid combining, especially at high doses. If using for sleep, choose one — not both.

herbs.effect: Additive sedation — Jatamansi affects GABA pathways similar to Zolpidem
herbs.recommendation: Avoid combination. Use Jatamansi alone for milder insomnia — discuss switching with doctor.
herbs.alternative: Jatamansi or Ashwagandha alone may suffice without Zolpidem for mild insomnia

herbs.effect: Additive sedation
herbs.recommendation: Avoid combining.

herbs.effect: Arjuna bark contains arjunic acid and other compounds with positive inotropic (cardiac glycoside-like) activity — additive effects with Digoxin risk toxicity. Digoxin has a narrow therapeutic window; small increases cause toxicity.
herbs.recommendation: STRICTLY AVOID Arjuna in any form while on Digoxin. This combination risks Digoxin toxicity (nausea, visual changes, arrhythmia).

herbs.effect: Guggulsterones may affect CYP3A4 — alters Digoxin clearance
herbs.recommendation: Avoid Guggulu on Digoxin. Monitor Digoxin levels if any cardiac herb is added.

herbs.effect: Licorice-induced hypokalemia (low potassium) dramatically increases Digoxin toxicity risk — hypokalemia sensitises the heart to Digoxin
herbs.recommendation: STRICTLY AVOID licorice in ANY form while on Digoxin.

herbs.effect: Both garlic and Amiodarone affect thyroid function; Amiodarone contains iodine and can cause thyroid dysfunction — garlic may compound this
herbs.recommendation: Inform cardiologist about garlic use. Monitor thyroid function regularly on Amiodarone.

herbs.effect: Both affect cardiac conduction — combination may cause bradycardia or arrhythmia
herbs.recommendation: AVOID Arjuna while on Amiodarone without cardiologist supervision.

herbs.effect: Red yeast rice contains monacolin K which is chemically identical to lovastatin — combined use is essentially double-dosing a statin. Risk of rhabdomyolysis (muscle breakdown) and liver toxicity.
herbs.recommendation: STRICTLY AVOID red yeast rice supplements while on any statin.
herbs.alternative: Guggulu for cholesterol with physician oversight

herbs.effect: Guggulsterones affect CYP3A4 which metabolises most statins — may raise statin blood levels and side effect risk
herbs.recommendation: Use with caution. Inform prescribing doctor. Monitor for muscle pain (myalgia).

herbs.effect: Neem may affect CYP3A4 — monitor for statin side effects
herbs.recommendation: Culinary use safe. Avoid Neem extract supplements on statins.

herbs.effect: Piperine (in Pippali and black pepper) is a potent CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein inhibitor — substantially increases Cyclosporine bioavailability and blood levels, risking nephrotoxicity and immunosuppressant toxicity
herbs.recommendation: STRICTLY AVOID all piperine-containing preparations (Trikatu, Pippali, Maricha in medicinal doses) while on Cyclosporine. Culinary pinch of black pepper in cooking generally safe — avoid concentrated supplements.

herbs.effect: Immunomodulatory activity of Ashwagandha may conflict with immunosuppression goal
herbs.recommendation: AVOID Ashwagandha in post-transplant immunosuppression. Discuss with transplant physician.

herbs.effect: High-dose curcumin affects CYP3A4 — may alter Cyclosporine levels
herbs.recommendation: Avoid curcumin supplements. Culinary turmeric (1/4 tsp) is acceptable.

herbs.effect: Ashwagandha's immunostimulatory activity directly opposes Tacrolimus immunosuppression
herbs.recommendation: AVOID Ashwagandha while on Tacrolimus (organ transplant setting). Discuss with transplant physician.

herbs.effect: CYP3A4 inhibition significantly increases Tacrolimus blood levels — nephrotoxicity risk
herbs.recommendation: STRICTLY AVOID Trikatu and medicinal piperine preparations on Tacrolimus.

herbs.effect: Both turmeric and Methotrexate have hepatotoxic potential — combined hepatotoxicity risk, especially with curcumin supplements > 1g/day
herbs.recommendation: Avoid curcumin supplements while on Methotrexate. Culinary turmeric acceptable. Monitor liver enzymes.

herbs.effect: Andrographis has immunomodulatory effects that may conflict with DMARD therapy
herbs.recommendation: Avoid Andrographis/Kalmegh during Methotrexate therapy.

herbs.effect: Antibiotics kill gut bacteria — probiotics (yogurt, fermented foods, Lactobacillus) help restore microbiome
herbs.recommendation: SEPARATE probiotics from antibiotic by at least 2 hours. Take probiotic 2 hours AFTER antibiotic. Continue probiotics for 2 weeks after completing antibiotic course.

herbs.effect: Garlic has synergistic antimicrobial activity — no harmful interaction. May support antibiotic effect.
herbs.recommendation: Safe to use culinary garlic during antibiotic therapy. Avoid high-dose supplements as they may irritate an antibiotic-stressed gut.

herbs.effect: No significant antibiotic interaction. Ashwagandha may support immunity during illness.
herbs.recommendation: Safe to continue during antibiotic therapy.

herbs.effect: Minor CYP2C9 inhibition — may slightly increase Fluconazole blood levels
herbs.recommendation: Culinary garlic acceptable. Avoid garlic supplements during Fluconazole treatment.

herbs.effect: Neem also has antifungal properties — potentially synergistic but interaction profile unclear
herbs.recommendation: Generally safe in culinary amounts. Avoid Neem extract supplements during antifungal treatment without medical supervision.

herbs.effect: Both are anti-inflammatory — additive effect may allow reduction of NSAID dose (beneficial) but also additive GI risk
herbs.recommendation: Monitor for GI symptoms (nausea, stomach pain). With doctor supervision, Guggulu may allow gradual NSAID dose reduction.

herbs.effect: Additive anti-inflammatory effect + additive antiplatelet activity. Combined may actually allow NSAID dose reduction.
herbs.recommendation: Inform prescriber. Culinary turmeric is safe. Curcumin supplements may allow NSAID reduction — discuss with doctor.
herbs.alternative: Turmeric + Boswellia (Shallaki) combination may reduce NSAID dependency — under medical guidance

herbs.effect: Ginger is anti-inflammatory + has GI-protective properties — may offset some NSAID GI side effects
herbs.recommendation: Ginger tea or culinary ginger with NSAID meals may help protect the stomach. Monitor for antiplatelet effects.

herbs.effect: Additive anti-inflammatory — may allow NSAID dose reduction in arthritis
herbs.recommendation: Safe to combine. May reduce NSAID dose requirement over time — discuss with doctor. Monitor for GI effects.

herbs.effect: Additive anti-inflammatory and antiplatelet effect
herbs.recommendation: Culinary amounts safe. Avoid high-dose curcumin supplements with Diclofenac.

herbs.effect: Additive antiplatelet activity
herbs.recommendation: Culinary amounts safe. Avoid supplements.

herbs.effect: High-dose garlic supplements significantly reduce Indinavir (protease inhibitor) AUC by up to 50% — major risk of treatment failure and HIV resistance development
herbs.recommendation: AVOID garlic supplements while on HIV protease inhibitors. Culinary garlic (1-2 cloves in cooking) considered acceptable by most HIV specialists.

herbs.effect: High-dose curcumin affects CYP3A4 which metabolises many antiretrovirals — may alter drug levels
herbs.recommendation: Avoid curcumin supplements > 500mg/day while on ARV therapy. Culinary turmeric acceptable.

herbs.effect: Ashwagandha may support immunity and reduce fatigue — no major antiretroviral interaction at standard doses
herbs.recommendation: Generally safe. Inform HIV care team.

herbs.effect: Shatavari has phytoestrogenic activity — may interact with hormonal OCP, though evidence is limited
herbs.recommendation: Inform gynecologist. Generally considered safe at standard Ayurvedic doses. Monitor for breakthrough bleeding.

herbs.effect: Ashoka has uterine tonic and mild estrogenic effects — theoretical interaction with OCPs
herbs.recommendation: Use with caution. Inform gynecologist.

herbs.effect: No significant OCP interaction
herbs.recommendation: Safe to use.

herbs.effect: Gokshura has diuretic properties — diuretics can raise Lithium blood levels dangerously (Lithium is excreted by kidneys alongside sodium)
herbs.recommendation: AVOID diuretic herbs (Punarnava, Gokshura) while on Lithium. Monitor Lithium levels carefully.

herbs.effect: Diuretic effect raises Lithium levels — toxicity risk
herbs.recommendation: AVOID while on Lithium.

herbs.effect: No significant Lithium interaction
herbs.recommendation: Safe to use at standard doses.

herbs.effect: DGL (deglycyrrhizinated licorice) is actually synergistic — classical and modern use for gastric protection
herbs.recommendation: DGL licorice is safe and beneficial with PPIs. Regular licorice (with glycyrrhizin) raises BP — avoid that form.

herbs.effect: Amla is gastroprotective — no adverse interaction with PPIs
herbs.recommendation: Safe to use. Amla may support gastric mucosal health.

herbs.effect: Take Triphala 2+ hours from PPI — tannins may affect absorption
herbs.recommendation: Separate by 2 hours. Night-time Triphala avoids interaction with morning PPI.

generalWarnings: DISCLOSURE RULE: Always inform every treating physician, pharmacologist, and pharmacist about ALL herbal supplements, Ayurvedic formulations, and home remedies you are taking. Many dangerous interactions are preventable with simple disclosure.

CYP450 ENZYME INTERACTIONS: Many herbs (Pippali, Garlic, Turmeric, Guggulu, Shankhapushpi) affect CYP450 liver enzymes that metabolise most pharmaceutical drugs. This can raise drug blood levels (toxicity risk) or lower them (treatment failure).

ADDITIVE vs ANTAGONISTIC: Interactions are not always harmful. Some herb-drug combinations are additive (e.g., Turmeric + NSAID may reduce NSAID dose needed). Others are antagonistic (e.g., Licorice counteracts antihypertensives). Severity matters — always check.

PREGNANCY AND NURSING: Many Ayurvedic herbs (Guggulu, Shankhapushpi, Ashoka, Pippali, Trikatu, concentrated Triphala) are contraindicated in pregnancy. Pregnant or nursing women must consult both their OB/GYN and an Ayurvedic physician before any herbal use.

KIDNEY AND LIVER DISEASE: Dose adjustments are critical in renal or hepatic impairment. Many herbs are Mutrala (diuretic) or Yakrit-acting (liver-acting) — safe doses in healthy individuals may be toxic in organ-impaired patients. Always disclose renal/liver status.

TIMING MATTERS: Many interactions are timing-dependent rather than absolute. Levothyroxine must be taken alone on empty stomach — herbs separate by 4+ hours. Probiotics must be separated from antibiotics by 2+ hours. Correct timing prevents most absorption-based interactions.

ANTICOAGULANT CAUTION — HIGH PRIORITY: Patients on Warfarin, Heparin, Aspirin, or Clopidogrel must avoid antiplatelet herbs (Garlic supplements, Ginger supplements, high-dose Turmeric, Amla supplements) without physician approval. These combinations can cause dangerous bleeding.

NARROW THERAPEUTIC WINDOW DRUGS: Digoxin, Lithium, Warfarin, Cyclosporine, Tacrolimus, Phenobarbital, and certain antiretrovirals have narrow therapeutic windows — small changes in blood levels cause toxicity or treatment failure. Be especially cautious with ANY herbal addition for patients on these drugs.

SHANKHAPUSHPI-PHENOBARBITAL INTERACTION: This is the most clinically documented and serious Ayurvedic herb-drug interaction. Shankhapushpi preparations (including Saraswatarishta) significantly reduce Phenobarbital blood levels and can cause seizure breakthrough. Never combine.

SELF-MEDICATION WARNING: Never add herbal supplements to manage a side effect of a prescription drug without telling your doctor. Example: adding Ashwagandha for fatigue while on SSRIs, or Punarnava for water retention while on diuretics, can create dangerous additive effects.
